title: What Is a Website, Really?
subtitle: Behind the scenes of every page you visit

sections.instruction: Walk through the 7 steps that happen every time you visit a website. Click "Next" to follow the journey.

steps.title: You type a URL
steps.description: You type something like "www.example.com" into your browser's address bar and press Enter. This URL is like a street address — it tells the internet where you want to go.

steps.title: DNS looks up the address
steps.description: Your browser asks a DNS (Domain Name System) server to translate "www.example.com" into a number like 93.184.216.34. Computers use these numbers (called IP addresses) to find each other, just like GPS uses coordinates instead of street names.

steps.title: Browser sends a request
steps.description: Your browser sends a message across the internet to the server at that IP address, saying: "Please send me the web page at this URL." This message is called an HTTP request.

steps.title: The server receives the request
steps.description: A computer (the server) somewhere in the world receives your request. It could be in a data center in Virginia, a warehouse in Dublin, or anywhere else on the planet.

steps.title: The server finds the files
steps.description: The server locates the HTML, CSS, JavaScript, and image files needed for that page. Sometimes it also pulls data from a database to build the page on the fly.

steps.title: The server sends a response
steps.description: The server packages up all those files and sends them back across the internet to your browser. This is the HTTP response.

steps.title: Your browser renders the page
steps.description: Your browser receives the files and assembles them into the page you see: HTML provides the structure, CSS makes it look good, and JavaScript makes it interactive. All of this happens in about 1-3 seconds.

sections.content:
Here's the key insight: **a website is just files on someone else's computer**.

When you visit a website, you're really just downloading files (HTML, CSS, JavaScript, images) from a computer somewhere in the world, and your browser assembles them into what you see on screen.

There's no magic. The "cloud" is just other people's computers. A website is just files. Your browser is just a file viewer that knows how to display them beautifully.
keyPoints: A website is files (HTML, CSS, JS, images) stored on a server
Your browser downloads those files and renders them into a page
The whole process takes about 1-3 seconds
The "cloud" is just computers in large buildings called data centers

sections.question: When you type a URL and press Enter, what is the first thing that happens?
options: The server sends you the web page immediately
Your browser translates the URL into an IP address using DNS
JavaScript starts running in your browser
The website downloads to your hard drive
sections.explanation: Before anything can happen, your browser needs to figure out which server to talk to. DNS translates the human-readable URL (like example.com) into a machine-readable IP address (like 93.184.216.34).

completionMessage: You now understand what happens behind the scenes every time you visit a website. It's not magic — it's just files being sent between computers. Next, we'll explore the difference between client and server.
